item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: As per here, the image was taken by Queen's University. As there is no indication that it was released under Creative Commons, it is assumed not compliant with the site's license and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting scip but I just don't get much out of it. I may have a slight bias against scips that mix silly and horrifying (remember these are 5 comatose women who are forced to 'birth' disembodied heads constantly) but I just don't like this one. A question I have is are the hosts also anomalous in regards to their biological needs? That is to say if they were comatose before containment who was taking care of them? To be honest I wouldn't put it past the Foundation to induce comas in all of them to both make containment easier and to spare them the horror of their existence. It's a no vote for me but if some pre containment context were present it would probably be +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assume everyone here reads enough creepy-pasta to know the general shape of the plot most of the time. At least enough to expect the Ancient Evil what speaks through a chorus of disembodied heads.

But this one turns our genre-savvy against us like a judo flip. You hit that last log like a staircase one step shorter than you thought it was; and the scare that isn't there is more off-putting and unsettling than any cosmic horror or murder ghost I can recall.

This absolutely deserves it's place in the +100 club.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was pretty sure it was a Pompeii mosaic photoshopped there. Can anyone confirm/deny?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jimmy, why did you mess with 5 sisters?

+1 for the madness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the interview log quite a bit.

The rest I don't find interesting. So no vote from me. (I had downvoted before I read the log, for the record, so that's something.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What might be interesting is a mixture of mundane stuff and less mundane stuff… you know.

300-odd verses of Vogon poetry, followed by… something odd… then another 300 verses of Vogon poetry…

But that's just my opinion. +1 anyway!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, I was gonna have the log be generic creepy ramblings a la SCP-058, but then I thought that something really stupid and mundane, like shitty poetry, would be pretty funny, and less boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I was like, "Meh". But then I read the log and it was so strange that I found myself really liking this. I mean, it makes no effing sense at all, and the lack of apparent reason behind his motive weakens it maybe a tiny bit. But there is something so charming here, breaking through the usual gloom and creepiness of SCP's, that I have to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that all of this weirdness builds up to some sort of… Thing, reading off first-year English Literature Major poetry via anomalous heads just makes it actually better in my book. No it's not scary or creepy (beyond the fact that it's a bunch of semi-malformed heads talking in ways they shouldn't) but it's just the right amount of what. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, nuts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edited.

im so good at consistent writing guys i swear

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the description you write that SCP-1263 consists of SCP-1263-1 through 5 and SCP-1263-A, but everywhere else in the article SCP-1263 seems to refer to just SCP-1263-1 to 5. Not a major issue.
This is fantastically strange, silly and horrible. Giving birth to apparently full-sized adult human heads? Poor women.
I'm not really sure though if an ultrasound image would show a face that clearly. From what I've seen they mostly show bones and some organs, looking more like aliens or skeletons, not really recognizable faces. None of my kids are that anomalous, though, or just heads, so I could be wrong and I'm willing to suspend my disbelief.
Great article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do not know what to think of this.

Have one upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removed. I'll just keep their life span ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I missed that sentence. Never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Instances of SCP-1263-A expire from abrupt cessation of brain function 5 months after spawning."

It's still there, and it's still a hole in the plot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if it was changed after you wrote this, but I don't see anything about the heads dying on their own. The only mention of death I see is us killing or keeping them at the discretion of the head (heh) researcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When at least 5 instances of SCP-1263-A spawned from the same member of SCP-1263 are brought into a minimum proximity of 1.4 meters near each other

I may have read it wrong, but how can you get five instances of 1263-A from the same 1263 in close proximity to eachother when they expire after 5 months, and the "gestation" is 3 months with a 1 month break?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just the words I was looking for. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If only I had the balls to write that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously Crappy Poems by Some Creative Penises

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somehow that's even more hilarious to me, as now I have the imagery of a gigantic testicle waxing horrible poetry at a confounded researcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you know that? Do I want to know how you know that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think anyone else had a problem with the wording. If they do, I'll reword it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so beautifully weird. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture is an ultrasound of a face in a man's testicle.

I repeat, the picture is an ultrasound of a man's testicle with a face on it.

And honestly, I think that that fact alone is more anomalous, and it kind of minimizes the effect of the article, because I know that picture is of a man's testicle.

EDIT: Here's *a* link. This was very popular a short time ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the thought streams of an unidentified individual, in Laotian.

the thoughts of a resident of Wisconsin named Ron Ackerson.

Might want to make up your mind.

EDIT: Or reword it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hahahaha! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Charmingly bizarre, I liked this quite a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was aiming for some silliness, actually.

Personally, I feel going for straight creep with no room for silliness would weaken the article, but that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the feeling of this, the pic creeps me out but the log is a little bit too silly. So we have someone who wants to communicate using heads, okay, that's creepy but also silly. I know it might be cliche but i would love to see something more disturbing about the incoming messages. Still upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll reword it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pregnant, verb. 1. (of a woman or female animal) Having a child or young developing in the uterus.
2. Having been in such a condition for a specified time: "she was six months pregnant".

If they're adult human hads that spontaneously appear, I don't think this is technically a pregnancy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This took longer than it's probably worth, but I needed to get the idea off my chest.
